value.Title: Lawmakers Grill CARB Over GHG Cap & Invest Proposal Ahead Of Vote

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-0TR3-RVX8-B0KF-00000-00Lawmakers Grill CARB Over GHG Cap & Invest Proposal Ahead Of Vote2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:39ZLexisNexisLawmakers Grill CARB Over GHG Cap & Invest Proposal Ahead Of Vote

May 7, 2026 California lawmakers at a special hearing grilled California Air Resources Board (CARB) officials over their proposal to ease industry compliance with the state's greenhouse gas cap-and-invest program, with the board poised to consider the controversial rule amendments near the end of this month.

"[I] am particularly concerned about the proposed amendments' potential impact on the Greenhouse Gas Reduction Fund" (GGRF), said Sen. Eloise Gomez Reyes (D-San Bernardino) during a May 6 joint hearing of the Senate Environmental Quality Committee and the Senate budget subcommittee on resources, environmental protection and energy.

"The GGRF has provided significant funding support for a wide range of important programs -- from affordable housing, to transit, wildfire prevention, safe drinking water, zero-emission transportation, and air quality improvements. If the proposed amendments are adopted, the funding level for these types of programs would be hollowed out. All to support certain industries for the supposed purpose of affordability," she added.

The hearing came after CARB on April 28 announced that it was extending the comment period on its proposed cap-and-invest program amendment revisions from April 29 to May 4, in response to a request by Reyes and Sen. Catherine Blakespear (D-Encinitas), who chairs the Senate environment committee.

Under pressure from industry, labor and some lawmakers, CARB on April 14 proposed revisions to ease the stringency of the program and provide industry with new monetary compliance assistance mechanisms.

Changes include "redirecting" $800 million in carbon allowances to support industry compliance through 2030 that otherwise would have been sold at the state's quarterly allowance auctions that fund the GGRF; and creating a first-time Manufacturing Decarbonization Incentive (MDI), which would provide $4 billion to support major industry projects to significantly reduce emissions at facilities by using clean alternatives, low-carbon hydrogen, renewable energy, and carbon capture and sequestration, among other technologies.

The MDI would be funded by CARB issuing "new incentive allowances in a separate reserve account to create the fund. The new allowances would be restricted to 118 million in total. These instruments would only be released into the market if a company applied for them to support investment in eligible emissions reductions projects," according to a CARB staff report.

But Reyes, Blakespear and other members of the Senate committees noted during the May 6 hearing that these programs could lower GGRF revenue by more than half -- from about $4 billion annually to possibly $1.8 billion. This would threaten the state's ability to not only pay for emission-reduction programs, but also to meet its 2030 GHG-reduction target of at least 40 percent below 1990 levels and potentially its 2045 net-zero carbon goal, they stressed.

They also noted that Gov. Gavin Newsom's (D) proposed fiscal year 2026-27 budget already cuts about $1 billion in GGRF funding that was agreed to in last year's fiscal plan.

'Big, Important Issue'

"So, on the budget level, this is a big, big important issue," said Sen. John Laird (D-Santa Cruz), who also chairs the Senate Budget & Fiscal Review Committee. "Given the history, I'm concerned about making sure this program works, in terms of having enough investment and significantly reducing emissions, and not rewarding people that might not be with the program in terms of reducing emissions."

Helen Kerstein, principal fiscal and policy analyst with the state's Legislative Analyst's Office, said during the hearing that by adding 118 million allowances, the MDI would allow total emissions to rise above the currently capped level established by CARB. This could jeopardize achievement of the state's 2030 GHG target, she said.

CARB Chair Lauren Sanchez defended the plan in response to numerous questions and concerns by lawmakers, saying board staff "tried to strike a delicate balance" between maintaining the proper stringency to the program while providing industry with compliance flexibility, to prevent higher costs on California residents and maintain "affordability" as per legislative direction.

While the board recognizes that GGRF revenue is very important, raising revenue is "not the primary goal" of the cap-and-invest program, she said. The main goal is to reduce GHG emissions cost-effectively while "minimizing leakage" of pollution and jobs to other states.

Reyes questioned Sanchez about whether CARB considered how significantly decreasing future GGRF revenue could affect programs currently funded by the GGRF.

Sanchez responded that it isn't CARB's role to allocate funding to different programs or identify viable funding sources to continue such programs. "I leave that to the legislature, Department of Finance and governor," she said.

Appropriate 'Price Signal'

More generally, Sanchez said the proposed changes will maintain an appropriate "price signal" to reduce GHG emissions to meet the state's goal -- including 11 percent annual reductions between now and 2030 -- while making the program more affordable.

In addition, by removing post-2030 allowance allocations from the proposal and addressing them in a planned follow-up rulemaking, CARB is giving the legislature more time to evaluate the market, economic uncertainties and how to properly balance the amount of allowances that go to industry, utilities and the state auctions, she said.

Reyes and Blakespear asked if CARB would consider delaying a vote on the proposal until June or later, but Sanchez and CARB Deputy Executive Officer Rajinder Sahota said they want to hold the vote at CARB's May 28 meeting, as currently scheduled, to ensure the new rules go into effect on Sept. 1, among other reasons.

Sen. Caroline Menjivar (D-San Fernando Valley) was especially critical of the proposal, peppering Sanchez and Sahota with numerous questions. She asserted the new plan would "bust" the overall emissions cap and slash investments via the GGRF.

Menjivar, Blakespear and Reyes also said they are very concerned that CARB's proposal omits mechanisms to ensure that companies' MDI projects will result in actual GHG cuts that would not have occurred otherwise, or whether the new amendments will reduce costs on California consumers.

Sanchez responded that CARB will have guardrails in place to ensure actual emission reductions, and it plans to closely track the program in terms of assuring industry cost savings are passed through to consumers.

Reyes replied: "I hope it's true, but it sounds like there is no assurance. . . . When you really study it, there are no guarantees. We talked about guardrails -- I don't see them." -- Curt Barry (cbarry@iwpnews.com)
